content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

November 5, 1993


Norman K. Sato
Chief Deputy City Attorney
City of San Jose
151 West Mission Street
San Jose, CA 95110

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-93-352

Dear Mr. Sato:

This is in response to your request for advice on behalf of
the City of San Jose and the San Jose Arena Authority (the
"Authority") under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act.1/ Since you are not seeking advice
concerning a specific decision before any public official, we are
treating your advice as a request for informal assistance.2/

QUESTIONS

1. If the City of San Jose receives tickets as part of its
contract to manage the City of San Jose Arena, and creates a
nonprofit authority specifically for the purpose of administering
the use of the tickets, would tickets given by the Authority to
state legislative official in accordance with the city's adopted
policy be a gift to the legislative official?

2. Would the answer to question 1 above be the same if the
City of San Jose, rather than the Authority issued the invitation?

3. Would the answer be the same if the City of San Jose or
the Authority invited elected officials from other local
jurisdictions?

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000 et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs. Section
18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660

Advice File No. I-93-352
Page Two


        4.  Would the answer be the same if the invited official paid
face value for the ticket?

        5.  What are the implications to the recipient of a gift
ticket from the city or the Authority?

                              CONCLUSIONS

        1.  Since the legislative official receiving the ticket is
not an official of the City of San Jose, the ticket would be a
gift to the legislative official.

        2.  Since the Authority was created specifically by the city
to administer the use of the tickets in accordance with the
adopted policy of the city, this answer would not differ if the
City of San Jose, rather than the Authority issued the invitation.

        3.  Since the local elected official from another
jurisdiction receiving the ticket is not an official of the City
of San Jose the ticket would be gift to the official.

        4.  If an official pays the face value for a ticket to attend
an event at the Arena, and the face value is that which would be
charged to members of the public, the official has not received a
gift.

        5.  State legislative officials and local elected officials
must disclose the receipt of gifts valued at $50 or more.  State
legislative officials are prohibited from receiving gifts valued
at $270 or more.  Local elected officials are prohibited from
receiving gifts valued at $1,000 or more.  In addition, public
officials are prohibited from making, or participating in the
making, of decisions which will have a material financial effect
on any source of a gift of $250 or more.

                              FACTS

        The City of San Jose has entered into a management agreement
for the city's new arena with the San Jose Management Corporation,
a California corporation.  As part of the terms of the agreement,
the city retains the use of a luxury box and a number of club
seats ("tickets").  The city has delegated the administration of
the use of these tickets to the San Jose Arena Authority, a
California non-profit corporation (the "Authority").  The
Authority was created by the city specifically for the purpose of
administering the distribution of the tickets.  The mayor appoints
all of the members of the board of directors of the Authority.

        The city has adopted a policy which regulates the use of the
passes to the Arena by the Authority.  Such use is not limited to
a public official of the City of San Jose and members of his or
her immediate family, but is open to any guest of the public
official as well.  In addition, the policy provides that the city

Advice File No. I-93-352
Page Three


may propose to the Authority that it make tickets available to
"appropriate recipients" who are participating in ceremonial
occasions, official welcoming of visiting dignitaries, economic
development outreach, and recognition for direct involvement in
city-related projects/programs.

ANALYSIS

The term "gift" is defined as any payment to the extent that
consideration of equal or greater value is not received3/ and
includes a rebate or discount in the price of anything of value
unless the rebate or discount is made in the regular course of
business to members of the public without regard to official
status. (Section 82028.)

Accordingly, when a public official receives a ticket to an
event, the public official has received a gift.

A gift of a pass or ticket which provides a one-time
admission to motion picture theaters, amusement parks, sporting
events, country clubs, etc., is valued at the face value of the
pass or ticket, provided that the face value is the price that
was, or otherwise would have been, offered to the general public.
(Regulation 18946.1.)

However, in 1977, the Commission determined that under some
circumstances a gift may be made to a city without providing any
significant or unusual benefit to a public official of the city.
Under such circumstances, the public official has no reporting
obligations since any benefit he or she receives, although free of
charge to the official and to the city, "would be analogous to
reimbursement for expenses or per diem from a state or local
government agency, items which are not reportable. Section
82030(b)(2)." (In re Stone (1977) 3 FPPC Ops. 52.)

The Commission codified the intent of Stone in Regulation
18726.7. That regulation provides that admission to motion
picture theaters, amusement parks, and similar places or events,
which are provided to an agency official are not gifts to the
official whenever:

(c) The tickets or passes are provided to the
agency as part of the contract for the use of the
facility and the distribution and use of the passes or
tickets are regulated by an officially adopted policy of
the agency.

Regulation 18726.7(c)


3/ If a public official pays equal of greater value for a ticket,
the public official has not received a gift.

Advice File No. I-93-352
Page Four


Both Stone and subsequent Regulation 18726.7 limit the
exception to gifts given to officials of the agency receiving the
tickets.

Even though the City of San Jose's policy allows tickets to
be available to appropriate recipients, if those appropriate
recipients are not public officials of the City of San Jose, those
recipients have received a gift from the city. If the recipients
are public officials subject to the provisions of the Act, the
officials have received a gift and may be subject to the gift
prohibitions under the Act. (Sections 89501 and 89504.)

Accordingly, if a public official for the city receives
tickets to an event at the Arena and brings a state legislative
official or local elected official of another jurisdiction as a
guest in accordance with the city's policy, the tickets will not
be gifts to the official of the city, but the ticket will be a
gift to the state legislative official or other local government
official who accompany the city official to the event.

The state legislative official or local elected official must
report receipt of a gift of $50 or more on their statement of
economic interests. (Section 87207.) In addition, the Act
prohibits public officials from making, or participating in
making, decisions which will have a material financial effect on
any source of a gift valued at $250 or more. (Sections 87102.5
and 87103.)

I trust this answers your questions sufficiently. If you
have any additional questions, or need further assistance, please
feel free to contact the Commission's Legal Division at (916) 322-
5901.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: Jeanette E. Turvill
Political Reform Consultant
Legal Division

SGC/JET/jt
